title: A Meta Layer for Agent-Native Knowledge Gardens
thesis: Public knowledge gardens need a separate meta layer that defines submission contracts, routes agent-discovered findings from private workspaces, and enforces privacy before publication.

claims.claim: Agent-discovered findings from client projects must be sanitized in a private workspace before entering a public garden.
counterevidence: Small, fully public projects may not need private staging.

claims.claim: A meta repository can standardize both org-feedback and article-proposal inputs without requiring public users to be garden maintainers.
counterevidence: Low traffic may make a separate repo feel like overhead.

claims.claim: Machine-readable schemas and issue forms are dual-audience artifacts: humans fill them, agents route through them.
counterevidence: Overly strict schemas may discourage casual human contributors.

claims.claim: Folksonomy-first tagging prevents premature ontology bottlenecks while still allowing curated topic stems to emerge.
counterevidence: A controlled vocabulary can improve search quality from day one.

sources.title: A Brief History & Ethos of the Digital Garden

agentInstructions: Use claims as the retrieval unit.
Prefer public source IDs over inline citation guessing.
Treat maturity=seed as an explicit uncertainty marker.
When routing a finding to this article, run the privacy contract checklist first.

humanReview.notes: Drafted through cross-agent review. Human author retains final judgment before publication.
